visual_checks: CRITICAL — LIST IDENTIFICATION: Scan the entire document for any content that visually appears as a list — bulleted items, numbered steps, lettered items, or any series of short items stacked vertically with markers or indentation.

For each visual list, check whether it uses a built-in list style/format or is constructed manually with typed characters (dashes, asterisks, hyphens, numbers followed by periods, letters followed by parentheses).

In PDFs, check the tag tree: proper lists use (list) containing
  • (list items), each with (label/marker) and (content). Manual fake lists will show as a series of

    tags.
In DOCX, check whether the List Bullet, List Number, or other list styles are used, or whether the author typed '1. ', '2. ', '- ' at the start of Normal-styled paragraphs.
Look for nested lists (sub-items indented under a parent item) and verify the nesting is structural, not just indentation on regular paragraphs.
Check for definition lists or glossary-style content (term followed by definition) that should use structured formatting.
Look for numbered procedures or instructions where the order matters — these must use ordered (numbered) list formatting.
pass_conditions: All bulleted content uses built-in bullet list styles or is tagged with <L> and <LI> in the PDF tag tree
All numbered/ordered content uses built-in numbered list styles or is tagged with ordered list tags in PDF
Nested lists are structurally nested (an <L> within an <LI>) rather than just visually indented
List markers (bullets, numbers, letters) are generated by the list style, not manually typed characters
Screen readers announce lists as lists (e.g., 'list of 5 items') and can navigate item by item
fail_conditions: Bulleted lists are created by typing dash (-), asterisk (*), hyphen, or other characters at the start of normal paragraphs instead of using list styles
Numbered lists are created by manually typing '1. ', '2. ', '3. ' etc. at the start of normal paragraphs instead of using numbered list styles

In PDFs, list content is tagged as a series of

tags instead of >

  • > structure
In DOCX, content that looks like a list uses Normal style with manual indentation and typed markers
Nested lists are simulated with tab characters or extra indentation on regular paragraphs instead of actual nested list formatting
Ordered lists use inconsistent or incorrect numbering because numbers are manually typed rather than auto-generated
A long document with multiple clearly visual lists has zero structural list formatting throughout
na_conditions: Document contains no content that visually appears as or functions as a list
Document is entirely prose paragraphs, tables, or other non-list content
ne_conditions: AI response could not be parsed
Document could not be opened or its list structure could not be inspected
Document format does not support structured list evaluation
common_mistakes: Typing a dash or asterisk followed by a space to create a 'bullet point' instead of using the Bullets button or list style — Word may auto-correct this, but not always

Manually numbering paragraphs ('1. First step', '2. Second step') instead of using a numbered list — when items are reordered, the numbers do not update and there is no list semantics for screen readers
Using indentation and line breaks to create the visual appearance of a list without any list formatting
Breaking a single list into multiple lists by applying the list style inconsistently (starting over instead of continuing)
Using a table with one column to simulate a list layout
Applying list formatting to content that is not actually a list (e.g., using bullet style on single standalone paragraphs for visual effect)

examples.pass: A Word document has a section titled 'Required Materials' followed by 8 items using the List Bullet style. A screen reader announces 'list of 8 items' and each item as 'bullet, [item text]'. The PDF export tags this as <L> with 8 <LI> children.
examples.fail:
A PDF procedures document has 15 numbered steps for completing a form. Each step is a

tag starting with a manually typed number ('1. ', '2. ', etc.). A screen reader reads these as regular paragraphs and cannot announce the total number of steps or navigate by list item.
examples.partial: A document has 6 bulleted lists. Four use proper List Bullet style, but two on page 3 were created by typing em-dashes followed by spaces on Normal-styled paragraphs.
